# Randomly generated content for the purpose of strings compare testing
Geothermal
energy
harnesses
heat
stored
beneath
the
Earth’s
surface
to
generate
electricity
and
provide
direct
heating.
This
energy
source
relies
on
naturally
occurring
temperature
gradients
created
by
the
planet’s
formation
and
radioactive
decay.
In
power
systems,
wells
are
drilled
to
access
hot
water
or
steam,
which
drives
turbines
connected
to
generators.
For
direct-use
applications,
geothermal
heat
can
warm
buildings,
greenhouses,
and
even
sidewalks
to
reduce
ice
buildup.
One
advantage
of
geothermal
systems
is
their
reliability,
as
subsurface
temperatures
remain
relatively
stable
year-round.
Initial
exploration
and
drilling
costs
can
be
high,
but
operating
expenses
are
typically
low
once
systems
are
established.
Advances
in
enhanced
geothermal
techniques
aim
to
expand
viability
beyond
naturally
hot
regions.
Overall,
geothermal
energy
offers
a
renewable
option
that
complements
other
clean
energy
sources
in
a
balanced
power
grid.
